title: Free Marketing Audit — Lead-Gen Landing Page
description: An original lead-magnet landing page sample for a fictional marketing agency — tests a low-friction, two-field form against a typical multi-field 'contact us' pattern.
longDescription: A self-initiated sample built around a lead-magnet pattern I've seen work well on real PPC pages: ask for almost nothing up front (just a URL and an email), promise a specific, fast deliverable (an audit report), and explain the process in three short steps so the visitor knows exactly what happens after they submit.
highlights: Two-field lead form (URL + email) instead of a full contact form, to lower the commitment ask
Three-step 'what happens next' section placed right after the form to reduce post-submit anxiety
Scarcity line ('Limited to 10 audits this week') used once, plainly, instead of countdown timers or pop-ups

caseStudy.problem: Generic 'Contact Us' lead-gen pages ask for too much too soon (name, company, phone, message) before the visitor has any reason to trust the business — that mismatch between ask and trust is what kills conversion on cold PPC traffic.
caseStudy.approach: Replaced the generic contact form with a specific, low-effort lead magnet: submit a URL and email, get a free audit back. The form sits beside the headline (not below a scroll), and a three-step process explainer answers 'what happens after I submit' immediately below it.
decisions: Cut the form to two fields — URL and email — since every additional field on a cold-traffic landing page is a drop-off point
Framed the offer as a specific deliverable ('a free audit report') instead of a vague 'get in touch', which gives the visitor a concrete reason to trust the exchange
Used a plain-text scarcity line instead of a countdown timer or modal, since artificial urgency widgets tend to read as untrustworthy on B2B-leaning traffic
caseStudy.outcome: // TODO: No real traffic — this is a self-initiated sample, not a deployed campaign. Conversion data not available.
